topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT411 - PECPT411
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** https://youtu.be/Y8FvvzcocT4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Transfer** is the branch of engineering thermodynamics and thermal science that deals with the **generation, use, conversion, and exchange of thermal energy (heat) between physical systems**. Heat transfer is classified into three fundamental modes: **Conduction, Convection, and Radiation**, all of which are governed by the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** (heat flows spontaneously from a higher temperature region to a lower temperature region).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the study of heat transfer is grounded in the **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction, Newton's Law of Cooling, and the Stefan-Boltzmann Law of Thermal Radiation**. These laws quantitatively relate the **heat flux** (rate of heat transfer per unit area) to the **temperature gradient** and the **thermophysical properties** of the medium involved.

### Mathematical Foundation of the Definition

The general rate equation for heat transfer is universally expressed as:

$$q = -k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$

Where:
- $q$ = Rate of heat transfer (W or J/s)
- $k$ = Thermal conductivity of the medium (W/m·K)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area perpendicular to heat flow (m²)
- $\frac{dT}{dx}$ = Temperature gradient in the direction of heat flow (K/m)

The negative sign in the equation indicates that heat flows **from higher to lower temperature**, ensuring compliance with the Second Law.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Crowd Moving Through a Narrow Gate**
>
> Imagine a packed cricket stadium (high energy/temperature region) trying to exit through a narrow turnstile gate (medium) into an empty parking lot (low energy/temperature region). The rate at which people exit depends on:
> 1. **How tightly packed the crowd is** → equivalent to the **temperature gradient**
> 2. **The width of the gate** → equivalent to the **cross-sectional area**
> 3. **The smoothness of the gate's rollers** → equivalent to the **thermal conductivity**

This analogy makes heat transfer immediately intuitive — students can visualize that a larger temperature difference, a wider path, and a more conductive material will all increase the rate of heat flow.

### Three Modes of Heat Transfer at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three modes of heat transfer — **Conduction, Convection, and Radiation** — are not mutually exclusive; in real-world engineering systems (such as a boiler, heat exchanger, or reactor), all three typically occur simultaneously. The dominant mode depends on the **medium, geometry, and operating temperature**.

| Mode | Carrier of Heat | Medium Required | Governing Law |
|------|----------------|-----------------|----------------|
| **Conduction** | Molecular vibration & electron motion | Solid, liquid, or gas | Fourier's Law |
| **Convection** | Bulk fluid motion | Fluid (liquid or gas) | Newton's Law of Cooling |
| **Radiation** | Electromagnetic waves | No medium required (vacuum OK) | Stefan-Boltzmann Law |

### Key Physical Constants Used in Heat Transfer

> [!NOTE]
> The following constants are **mandatory for KTU numerical problems**:
> - **Stefan-Boltzmann constant** ($\sigma$) = $5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ **W/m²·K⁴**
> - **Universal Gas Constant** ($R$) = **8.314 J/mol·K**
> - **Absolute zero** = **0 K = −273.15 °C**
> - **Standard atmospheric pressure** = **101.325 kPa**

These constants are highlighted in **bold** because KTU examiners frequently test them directly in Part A questions (3-mark short-answer items) within Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mode 1: Conduction (Fourier's Law)

### Operational Logic Step-by-Step

Conduction is the transfer of heat through a material **without bulk motion of the medium**. Heat is transferred via:
1. **Lattice vibrations (phonons)** in non-metallic solids
2. **Free electron drift** in metallic solids (the dominant mechanism in pure metals)
3. **Molecular collisions** in fluids (liquids and gases)

The rate of conductive heat transfer through a plane wall is governed by **Fourier's Law**, expressed for one-dimensional steady-state heat conduction as:

$$q_x = -k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$

For a plane wall of thickness $L$ with surface temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$, integrating the differential form yields the **integrated form**:

$$q = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Conductivity (k)** is the defining material property for conduction. It has units of **W/m·K** and varies significantly across materials:
> - **Pure metals**: Silver (~429 W/m·K), Copper (~401 W/m·K), Aluminum (~237 W/m·K) — **very high k**
> - **Non-metallic solids**: Glass (~1.4 W/m·K), Asbestos (~0.08 W/m·K) — **low k**
> - **Liquids**: Water (~0.6 W/m·K), Engine oil (~0.15 W/m·K) — **moderate k**
> - **Gases**: Air (~0.026 W/m·K), Hydrogen (~0.18 W/m·K) — **very low k**

### Why Does Conduction Occur?

Conduction occurs because of the **temperature gradient**. When one end of a solid is heated, the molecules/electrons at the hot end gain kinetic energy and transfer it to neighboring particles via collisions. This chain reaction propagates the thermal energy from the hot side to the cold side until thermal equilibrium is reached.

### Thermal Resistance Concept

The conduction heat transfer equation can be rewritten in the form of an electrical analogy (Ohm's Law analog):

$$q = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{th}}$$

Where $R_{th}$ is the **thermal resistance** of the wall:

$$R_{th} = \frac{L}{k \cdot A}$$

This analogy is extremely useful in KTU problems involving **composite walls** and **multi-layered cylinders**, where thermal resistances add up in series (similar to electrical resistors in series).

## 2.2 Mode 2: Convection (Newton's Law of Cooling)

### Operational Logic Step-by-Step

Convection is the transfer of heat between a **solid surface** and a **fluid in motion** (liquid or gas). It combines:
1. **Conduction** at the fluid-surface interface (within a thin stagnant boundary layer)
2. **Advection** by bulk fluid motion (energy carried by fluid parcels)

The rate of convective heat transfer is governed by **Newton's Law of Cooling**:

$$q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$$

Where:
- $h$ = Convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = Surface area in contact with fluid (m²)
- $T_s$ = Surface temperature (K or °C)
- $T_\infty$ = Bulk fluid temperature far from surface (K or °C)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The convective heat transfer coefficient $h$ is **not a true material property** — it depends on:
> - **Fluid type** (water, air, oil, etc.)
> - **Flow regime** (laminar, transitional, or turbulent)
> - **Flow geometry** (flow over a flat plate, inside a pipe, across a cylinder, etc.)
> - **Surface roughness** and **temperature difference**
>
> Typical ranges of $h$:
> - **Natural convection in gases**: $h$ ≈ 2 to 25 W/m²·K
> - **Natural convection in liquids**: $h$ ≈ 50 to 1000 W/m²·K
> - **Forced convection in gases**: $h$ ≈ 25 to 250 W/m²·K
> - **Forced convection in liquids**: $h$ ≈ 100 to 20,000 W/m²·K
> - **Boiling/Condensation**: $h$ ≈ 2,500 to 100,000 W/m²·K

### The Nusselt Number — The Heart of Convection Analysis

In KTU problems, $h$ is rarely measured directly. Instead, it is obtained from the **dimensionless Nusselt number (Nu)**:

$$Nu = \frac{h \cdot L}{k_f}$$

Where $L$ is the **characteristic length** and $k_f$ is the **thermal conductivity of the fluid**. Empirical correlations (like the **Dittus-Boelter equation** for turbulent pipe flow) give Nu as a function of the **Reynolds number (Re)** and the **Prandtl number (Pr)**:

$$Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{n}$$

Where $n = 0.4$ for heating and $n = 0.3$ for cooling of the fluid.

## 2.3 Mode 3: Radiation (Stefan-Boltzmann Law)

### Operational Logic Step-by-Step

Radiation is the transfer of heat via **electromagnetic waves** (primarily in the infrared spectrum for engineering temperatures). Unlike conduction and convection, radiation:
1. **Does not require a medium** — it can occur in a perfect vacuum
2. **Travels at the speed of light** ($c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s)
3. **Follows the Stefan-Boltzmann Law** for a perfect blackbody:

$$q = \sigma \cdot A \cdot T^4$$

Where:
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ (Stefan-Boltzmann constant)
- $A$ = Surface area of the radiating body (m²)
- $T$ = Absolute temperature of the surface (K)

For a **real surface** (called a *graybody*), the emissivity $\epsilon$ is introduced:

$$q = \epsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot T^4$$

### Net Radiation Exchange Between Two Bodies

In most engineering problems, the **net radiative heat exchange** between a hot body (surface 1) at $T_1$ and a surrounding body (surface 2) at $T_2$ is given by:

$$q_{net} = \sigma \cdot A \cdot (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$$

For a small object inside a large enclosure, this expression directly applies. For two finite parallel surfaces, a **view factor** (also called a *configuration factor* or *geometric factor*) $F_{1 \to 2}$ must be included:

$$q_{net} = A_1 \cdot F_{1 \to 2} \cdot \sigma \cdot (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Emissivity ($\epsilon$)** is a surface property:
> - **Polished silver**: $\epsilon \approx 0.02$ (excellent reflector, poor emitter)
> - **Polished aluminum**: $\epsilon \approx 0.05$
> - **Oxidized steel**: $\epsilon \approx 0.80$
> - **Black paint (matte)**: $\epsilon \approx 0.98$ (essentially a blackbody)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **highest-priority formula compilation** for solving Module 2 problems in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE. Memorize these expressions — they appear in over **90% of past-year Module 2 heat transfer problems**.

| # | Mode / Concept | Governing Equation | Units | Key Variables |
|---|----------------|--------------------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | Fourier's Law (1D Steady Conduction) | $q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{L}$ | W | $k$ (W/m·K), $A$ (m²), $\Delta T$ (K), $L$ (m) |
| 2 | Conduction Thermal Resistance | $R_{th} = \dfrac{L}{k \cdot A}$ | K/W | $L$, $k$, $A$ |
| 3 | Conduction Through Cylinder | $q = \dfrac{2 \pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | W | $r_1$, $r_2$ = inner, outer radii |
| 4 | Conduction Through Sphere | $q = \dfrac{4 \pi k (T_1 - T_2)}{(1/r_1 - 1/r_2)}$ | W | $r_1$, $r_2$ |
| 5 | Newton's Law of Cooling (Convection) | $q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$ | W | $h$ (W/m²·K) |
| 6 | Nusselt Number | $Nu = \dfrac{h \cdot L}{k_f}$ | dimensionless | $L$, $k_f$ |
| 7 | Dittus-Boelter Correlation | $Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^n$ | dimensionless | $Re$, $Pr$ |
| 8 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | $\rho$, $v$, $D$, $\mu$ |
| 9 | Prandtl Number | $Pr = \dfrac{\mu \cdot c_p}{k}$ | dimensionless | $\mu$, $c_p$, $k$ |
| 10 | Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Blackbody) | $q = \sigma A T^4$ | W | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ |
| 11 | Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Graybody) | $q = \epsilon \sigma A T^4$ | W | $\epsilon$ (emissivity) |
| 12 | Net Radiation Exchange | $q_{net} = \sigma A (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | W | $T_1$, $T_2$ in Kelvin |
| 13 | Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{L}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | K/W | For composite walls |
| 14 | Critical Radius of Insulation | $r_{cr} = \dfrac{k}{h_o}$ | m | Insulation criterion |
| 15 | Fin Efficiency | $\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{q_{actual}}{q_{max}}$ | dimensionless | Fin heat transfer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Temperature Reminder**: All temperature values used in radiation formulas **must be in Kelvin (K)**. Failure to convert from Celsius is a top reason for losing marks in KTU exams. Use $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Heat transfer analysis is fundamental to nearly every engineering discipline:
> - **Chemical Engineering**: Design of shell-and-tube heat exchangers, condensers, reboilers, and reactor cooling systems
> - **Mechanical Engineering**: Internal combustion engines, gas turbines, HVAC systems, refrigeration cycles
> - **Civil Engineering**: Building insulation, thermal comfort studies, concrete curing heat removal
> - **Electrical Engineering**: Cooling of semiconductor devices, transformer thermal management
> - **Aerospace Engineering**: Re-entry vehicle thermal protection, jet engine combustion chambers

The **overall heat transfer coefficient (U)** is the single most-used parameter in industrial heat exchanger design, and its derivation via the **thermal resistance network** is a frequent KTU exam question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Conduction Through a Plane Wall (Fourier's Law Integration)

### Starting Premise

Consider a plane wall of thickness $L$ (in meters), thermal conductivity $k$ (assumed constant), and cross-sectional area $A$. The two faces of the wall are maintained at fixed temperatures $T_1$ (hot side) and $T_2$ (cold side), with $T_1 > T_2$. Steady-state, one-dimensional heat conduction is assumed.

### Step 1: State Fourier's Law in Differential Form

$$q_x = -k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$

This equation states that the rate of heat flow in the $x$-direction is proportional to the negative temperature gradient.

### Step 2: Separate Variables

Rearranging Fourier's Law to separate $T$ and $x$:

$$q_x \cdot dx = -k \cdot A \cdot dT$$

### Step 3: Apply Steady-State Condition

Under steady state, $q_x$ does not vary with position $x$ (otherwise heat would accumulate). Thus, $q_x = q = \text{constant}$.

### Step 4: Integrate Both Sides

Integrate from $x = 0$ (where $T = T_1$) to $x = L$ (where $T = T_2$):

$$\int_{0}^{L} q \, dx = -k \cdot A \cdot \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

### Step 5: Evaluate the Integrals

$$\begin{aligned}
q \cdot [x]_{0}^{L} &= -k \cdot A \cdot [T]_{T_1}^{T_2} \\
q \cdot (L - 0) &= -k \cdot A \cdot (T_2 - T_1) \\
q \cdot L &= k \cdot A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Solve for q

$$\boxed{q = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}{L}}$$

This is the final integrated form of Fourier's Law for one-dimensional, steady-state conduction through a plane wall.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Interpretation**: The rate of heat transfer $q$ is **directly proportional** to the thermal conductivity $k$, cross-sectional area $A$, and temperature difference $\Delta T = (T_1 - T_2)$. It is **inversely proportional** to the wall thickness $L$. This is why insulation materials are designed to have **low k** and are applied in **large thicknesses** to reduce conductive heat loss.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Conduction Through a Composite Wall (Multi-Layer Resistance Network)

### Starting Premise

Consider a composite wall consisting of three layers (e.g., brick + insulation + plaster) with thicknesses $L_1, L_2, L_3$ and thermal conductivities $k_1, k_2, k_3$. The outer surfaces are at $T_1$ (hot) and $T_4$ (cold). Each interface temperature is $T_2$ and $T_3$ respectively.

### Step 1: Apply Fourier's Law to Each Layer

For steady-state, the heat flux through each layer is the same (conservation of energy, no heat accumulation):

$$\begin{aligned}
q &= \frac{k_1 \cdot A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}{L_1} \\
q &= \frac{k_2 \cdot A \cdot (T_2 - T_3)}{L_2} \\
q &= \frac{k_3 \cdot A \cdot (T_3 - T_4)}{L_3}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Express Each Layer as a Thermal Resistance

Divide through by $q$ in each equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{T_1 - T_2}{q} &= \frac{L_1}{k_1 \cdot A} = R_1 \\
\frac{T_2 - T_3}{q} &= \frac{L_2}{k_2 \cdot A} = R_2 \\
\frac{T_3 - T_4}{q} &= \frac{L_3}{k_3 \cdot A} = R_3
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Add All Three Equations

The left-hand sides add algebraically:

$$\frac{(T_1 - T_2) + (T_2 - T_3) + (T_3 - T_4)}{q} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3$$

The intermediate temperatures cancel:

$$\frac{T_1 - T_4}{q} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3$$

### Step 4: Solve for q

$$\boxed{q = \frac{A \cdot (T_1 - T_4)}{\frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2} + \frac{L_3}{k_3}} = \frac{\Delta T_{overall}}{\sum R_{th}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: This derivation is essentially an **analogy to Kirchhoff's Voltage Law** in electrical circuits. Thermal resistances in series add up just like electrical resistances, and the heat flux acts like current. The temperature difference is analogous to voltage drop.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient for a Composite Wall with Convection

### Starting Premise

Consider a wall with two convective boundaries — fluid on the inside (with $h_i$ and $T_{\infty,i}$) and fluid on the outside (with $h_o$ and $T_{\infty,o}$). The wall itself has thickness $L$ and thermal conductivity $k$.

### Step 1: Write Heat Transfer Rate for Each Resistance

$$\begin{aligned}
q &= h_i \cdot A \cdot (T_{\infty,i} - T_{s,i}) \\
q &= \frac{k \cdot A \cdot (T_{s,i} - T_{s,o})}{L} \\
q &= h_o \cdot A \cdot (T_{s,o} - T_{\infty,o})
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Express Each as a Resistance

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\infty,i} - T_{s,i} &= \frac{q}{h_i \cdot A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_i = \frac{1}{h_i \cdot A} \\
T_{s,i} - T_{s,o} &= \frac{q \cdot L}{k \cdot A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_{wall} = \frac{L}{k \cdot A} \\
T_{s,o} - T_{\infty,o} &= \frac{q}{h_o \cdot A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_o = \frac{1}{h_o \cdot A}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Sum the Resistances

$$T_{\infty,i} - T_{\infty,o} = q \cdot (R_i + R_{wall} + R_o)$$

### Step 4: Define Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient U

By definition:

$$q = U \cdot A \cdot (T_{\infty,i} - T_{\infty,o})$$

This gives:

$$U = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{L}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o}}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{L}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance**: The **lowest value among $h_i$, $L/k$, and $h_o$** dominates the overall resistance. For example, if $h_o$ (outside air, natural convection) is very small, the wall's thermal performance is controlled by outside convection regardless of how thick the insulation is. This is the principle behind **selective surface engineering** and **finned tubes**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Critical Radius of Insulation

### Starting Premise

Consider a cylindrical pipe of outer radius $r_1$ covered with a layer of insulation of outer radius $r_2$. The outer surface is exposed to convection with coefficient $h$. We want to find the value of $r_2$ that **maximizes** the heat loss.

### Step 1: Heat Loss Through Insulation + Outer Convection

For a cylinder of length $L$, the total resistance per unit length is:

$$R_{total} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2 \pi k L} + \frac{1}{h \cdot 2 \pi r_2 L}$$

### Step 2: Heat Loss Expression

$$q = \frac{T_1 - T_\infty}{R_{total}} = \frac{2 \pi L (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \frac{1}{h \cdot r_2}}$$

### Step 3: Find Maximum Heat Loss by Taking Derivative

To maximize $q$, we need to minimize $R_{total}$. Differentiating $R_{total}$ with respect to $r_2$ and setting it to zero:

$$\frac{d R_{total}}{d r_2} = \frac{1}{2 \pi k L} \cdot \frac{1}{r_2} - \frac{1}{h \cdot 2 \pi L r_2^2} = 0$$

### Step 4: Solve for r₂

$$\frac{1}{k \cdot r_2} = \frac{1}{h \cdot r_2^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_2 = \frac{k}{h}$$

### Step 5: Define Critical Radius

$$\boxed{r_{cr} = \frac{k_{insulation}}{h_{external}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counterintuitive Result**: Adding insulation to a small-radius pipe can actually **increase** the heat loss until the outer radius exceeds the **critical radius**. After $r_2 > r_{cr}$, adding more insulation reduces heat loss as expected. This is why insulation is effective on **large-diameter pipes** but may backfire on **thin electrical wires** or **small sensor probes**.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define thermal conductivity. State its SI unit and explain the physical significance of a high thermal conductivity for an engineering material.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition**: Thermal conductivity ($k$) is defined as the **rate of heat transfer through a unit thickness of a material per unit area per unit temperature difference**.

$$k = \frac{q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta T}$$

**SI Unit**: W/m·K (Watts per meter-Kelvin)

**Physical Significance**:
- A material with **high thermal conductivity** (e.g., copper, aluminum) allows heat to flow through it **very easily**, making it ideal for **heat dissipation applications** such as heat sinks in electronics, boiler tubes, and cooking utensils.
- A material with **low thermal conductivity** (e.g., asbestos, polyurethane foam) **resists heat flow**, making it ideal for **thermal insulation** in buildings, pipelines, and cryogenic vessels.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**State Newton's Law of Cooling and Stefan-Boltzmann Law. Why are temperatures required in Kelvin for radiation calculations?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newton's Law of Cooling**: The rate of convective heat transfer between a surface and a fluid is directly proportional to the temperature difference between them and the surface area in contact.

$$q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$$

Where $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K).

**Stefan-Boltzmann Law**: The total radiant heat energy emitted per unit time per unit area of a blackbody is directly proportional to the **fourth power of its absolute temperature**.

$$q = \sigma \cdot A \cdot T^4$$

Where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴.

**Why Kelvin?**:
- The Stefan-Boltzmann Law is derived from Planck's distribution and quantum statistical mechanics, which is based on **absolute thermodynamic temperature**.
- If Celsius is used directly, the $T^4$ term becomes incorrect because it would include an arbitrary zero point (e.g., at $0°$C, $T^4 = 0$ in Celsius, but radiation is still significant since $T = 273.15$ K).
- Using **absolute zero (0 K)** as the reference point ensures thermodynamic consistency with the Second Law.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Standard Choice
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / KTU Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

A furnace wall consists of three layers:
1. **Inner fireclay brick**: Thickness $L_1 = 0.10$ m, $k_1 = 1.0$ W/m·K
2. **Middle insulating brick**: Thickness $L_2 = 0.15$ m, $k_2 = 0.20$ W/m·K
3. **Outer red brick**: Thickness $L_3 = 0.075$ m, $k_3 = 0.72$ W/m·K

The inside surface temperature is $T_{s,i} = 800$ K and the outside surface temperature is $T_{s,o} = 350$ K. The wall area is $A = 3.5$ m².

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]**: Calculate the steady-state heat loss through the wall per hour.

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]**: Find the temperature at the interface between the fireclay and insulating bricks, and verify whether the outer brick is the dominant resistance.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

A horizontal cylindrical electrical wire of diameter $D = 5$ mm and surface temperature $T_s = 80°$C is exposed to still air at $T_\infty = 25°$C. The emissivity of the wire surface is $\epsilon = 0.65$.

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]**: If only natural convection occurs with $h = 12$ W/m²·K, calculate the heat loss per unit length of wire.

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]**: If radiation is also considered (with surrounding enclosure temperature $T_{surr} = 30°$C), calculate the total heat loss per unit length and the percentage contribution of radiation.

---

### Complete Step-by-Step Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a): Heat Loss Through Composite Wall [7 Marks]

**Step 1: List the Given Data**

$$L_1 = 0.10 \text{ m}, \quad k_1 = 1.0 \text{ W/m·K}$$
$$L_2 = 0.15 \text{ m}, \quad k_2 = 0.20 \text{ W/m·K}$$
$$L_3 = 0.075 \text{ m}, \quad k_3 = 0.72 \text{ W/m·K}$$
$$T_{s,i} = 800 \text{ K}, \quad T_{s,o} = 350 \text{ K}, \quad A = 3.5 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Thermal Resistance of Each Layer**

The thermal resistance formula is $R_i = \dfrac{L_i}{k_i \cdot A}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= \frac{0.10}{1.0 \times 3.5} = \frac{0.10}{3.5} = 0.02857 \text{ K/W} \\
R_2 &= \frac{0.15}{0.20 \times 3.5} = \frac{0.15}{0.70} = 0.21429 \text{ K/W} \\
R_3 &= \frac{0.075}{0.72 \times 3.5} = \frac{0.075}{2.52} = 0.02976 \text{ K/W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Total Thermal Resistance**

Since the resistances are in series:

$$R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = 0.02857 + 0.21429 + 0.02976 = 0.27262 \text{ K/W}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Heat Loss Rate**

$$q = \frac{T_{s,i} - T_{s,o}}{R_{total}} = \frac{800 - 350}{0.27262}$$

$$q = \frac{450}{0.27262} = 1650.8 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5: Convert to Heat Loss per Hour**

$$Q = q \times 3600 = 1650.8 \times 3600 = 5{,}942{,}880 \text{ J/h}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 5.943 \text{ MJ/h} \quad \text{or} \quad 1650.8 \text{ W}}$$

**Marks Distribution [7 Marks]**:
- (1 mark) — Correct formula for thermal resistance
- (2 marks) — Correct calculation of all three resistances
- (1 mark) — Correct summation
- (2 marks) — Correct final heat loss calculation
- (1 mark) — Proper unit conversion and final answer

---

#### Part (b): Interface Temperature and Dominant Resistance [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Find Interface Temperature T₂ (between fireclay and insulating brick)**

Since heat flux $q$ is the same through all layers:

$$q = \frac{T_{s,i} - T_2}{R_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_2 = T_{s,i} - q \cdot R_1$$

$$T_2 = 800 - 1650.8 \times 0.02857$$

$$T_2 = 800 - 47.16 = 752.84 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2: Find Interface Temperature T₃ (between insulating and outer brick)**

$$T_3 = T_2 - q \cdot R_2$$

$$T_3 = 752.84 - 1650.8 \times 0.21429$$

$$T_3 = 752.84 - 353.74 = 399.10 \text{ K}$$

**Verification**: Check $T_3 - T_{s,o} = q \cdot R_3$:

$$399.10 - 350 = 49.10 \text{ K} \quad \text{vs} \quad 1650.8 \times 0.02976 = 49.13 \text{ K} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3: Determine Dominant Resistance**

Compute percentage contribution of each resistance:

$$\begin{aligned}
\%R_1 &= \frac{0.02857}{0.27262} \times 100\% = 10.48\% \\
\%R_2 &= \frac{0.21429}{0.27262} \times 100\% = 78.61\% \\
\%R_3 &= \frac{0.02976}{0.27262} \times 100\% = 10.91\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion**: The **middle insulating brick layer** ($R_2 = 78.61\%$) is the **dominant resistance**, as expected because it has the lowest thermal conductivity ($k_2 = 0.20$ W/m·K). The outer red brick contributes only ~10.9%, so it is **not** the dominant resistance.

$$\boxed{T_2 = 752.84 \text{ K}, \quad T_3 = 399.10 \text{ K}, \quad R_2 \text{ is dominant at } 78.61\%}$$

**Marks Distribution [7 Marks]**:
- (2 marks) — Correct calculation of $T_2$
- (2 marks) — Correct calculation of $T_3$ with verification
- (2 marks) — Correct percentage analysis and identification of dominant resistance
- (1 mark) — Final conclusion with proper engineering reasoning

---

### Complete Step-by-Step Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a): Convective Heat Loss per Unit Length [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Identify Given Data**

$$D = 5 \text{ mm} = 0.005 \text{ m}, \quad L = 1 \text{ m (per unit length)}$$
$$T_s = 80°\text{C} = 353.15 \text{ K}, \quad T_\infty = 25°\text{C} = 298.15 \text{ K}$$
$$h = 12 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Surface Area per Unit Length**

For a cylinder of length $L = 1$ m:

$$A = \pi \cdot D \cdot L = \pi \times 0.005 \times 1 = 0.01571 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Apply Newton's Law of Cooling**

$$q_{conv} = h \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$$

$$q_{conv} = 12 \times 0.01571 \times (353.15 - 298.15)$$

$$q_{conv} = 12 \times 0.01571 \times 55 = 10.37 \text{ W per meter}$$

$$\boxed{q_{conv} \approx 10.37 \text{ W/m}}$$

**Marks Distribution [7 Marks]**:
- (1 mark) — Area calculation
- (1 mark) — Proper unit conversion (mm to m, °C to K)
- (3 marks) — Correct application of Newton's Law
- (2 marks) — Final numerical answer with units

---

#### Part (b): Radiation + Total Heat Loss [7 Marks]

**Step 1: List Radiation Parameters**

$$T_s = 353.15 \text{ K}, \quad T_{surr} = 30°\text{C} = 303.15 \text{ K}$$
$$\epsilon = 0.65, \quad \sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}^4$$

**Step 2: Calculate Radiative Heat Loss per Unit Length**

$$q_{rad} = \epsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot (T_s^4 - T_{surr}^4)$$

Compute $T_s^4$ and $T_{surr}^4$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_s^4 &= (353.15)^4 = 1.554 \times 10^{10} \text{ K}^4 \\
T_{surr}^4 &= (303.15)^4 = 8.444 \times 10^{9} \text{ K}^4
\end{aligned}$$

Now substitute:

$$q_{rad} = 0.65 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.01571 \times (1.554 \times 10^{10} - 8.444 \times 10^{9})$$

$$q_{rad} = 0.65 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.01571 \times 7.096 \times 10^{9}$$

$$q_{rad} = 0.65 \times 5.67 \times 0.01571 \times 70.96 = 4.117 \text{ W/m}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Total Heat Loss**

$$q_{total} = q_{conv} + q_{rad} = 10.37 + 4.117 = 14.49 \text{ W/m}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Percentage Contribution of Radiation**

$$\% \text{Radiation} = \frac{q_{rad}}{q_{total}} \times 100\% = \frac{4.117}{14.49} \times 100\%$$

$$\% \text{Radiation} = 28.41\%$$

$$\boxed{q_{rad} = 4.12 \text{ W/m}, \quad q_{total} = 14.49 \text{ W/m}, \quad \text{Radiation contributes } 28.41\%}$$

**Marks Distribution [7 Marks]**:
- (2 marks) — Correct temperature conversions to Kelvin
- (2 marks) — Correct application of Stefan-Boltzmann Law with emissivity
- (2 marks) — Correct total heat loss summation
- (1 mark) — Correct percentage calculation

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Heat Transfer Problems — KTU 2024 Scheme Examinations**
>
> **Pitfall 1**: Using **Celsius temperatures directly** in radiation formulas. This is the #1 reason students lose marks. Always convert to Kelvin: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$. Failure to do so will cost **2-3 marks** per question.
>
> **Pitfall 2**: **Unit inconsistency**. Thermal conductivity in W/m·K, thickness in meters, area in m². Mixing mm with m, or kW with W, results in answers that are **off by factors of 1000**. Always write down units at each step.
>
> **Pitfall 3**: **Forgetting the Stefan-Boltzmann constant value**. Students often write $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ but forget to multiply correctly. Memorize it as **"5.67 × 10 raised to minus 8"**.
>
> **Pitfall 4**: **Adding thermal resistances incorrectly**. In parallel arrangements (e.g., fins, parallel cylinders), use reciprocal addition: $\frac{1}{R_{total}} = \sum \frac{1}{R_i}$. In series, simply add: $R_{total} = \sum R_i$. Mixing these up is a common error.
>
> **Pitfall 5**: **Emissivity confusion**. Some students confuse $\epsilon$ with view factor $F$, or forget to include $\epsilon$ in the Stefan-Boltzmann formula for graybody radiation. The emissivity is a property of the **surface material**, not the geometry.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 Heat Transfer Fundamentals**
>
> ✅ **Three Modes of Heat Transfer**: Conduction (Fourier), Convection (Newton), Radiation (Stefan-Boltzmann) — all can occur simultaneously in real systems.
>
> ✅ **Fourier's Law**: $q = kA\Delta T/L$ — memorize the integrated form; $k$ has units W/m·K.
>
> ✅ **Thermal Resistance Network**: For series layers, add resistances directly ($R_{total} = \sum L_i/k_iA$). For parallel paths, use reciprocals. The **largest resistance dominates** the heat transfer.
>
> ✅ **Newton's Law of Cooling**: $q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$ — $h$ depends on flow regime, fluid type, and geometry; not a true material property.
>
> ✅ **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**: $q = \epsilon \sigma A T^4$ — always use **Kelvin**, never Celsius. $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴.
>
> ✅ **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient**: $\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{L}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o}$ — critical for heat exchanger design.
>
> ✅ **Critical Radius of Insulation**: $r_{cr} = k/h$ — for cylindrical pipes, insulation increases heat loss until $r_2 > r_{cr}$.
>
> ✅ **Nusselt Number**: $Nu = hL/k_f$ — dimensionless, used to calculate $h$ from empirical correlations.
>
> ✅ **Dittus-Boelter Correlation** (for turbulent flow in pipes): $Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}$ (heating) or $Pr^{0.3}$ (cooling).
>
> ✅ **Temperature Conversion**: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$ — **always mandatory for radiation** problems.
>
> ✅ **Common Materials' k-Values**: Copper (401), Aluminum (237), Steel (50), Glass (1.4), Water (0.6), Air (0.026), Insulation (0.04) W/m·K.
>
> ✅ **Emissivity Values**: Polished metals (0.02-0.1), Painted surfaces (0.9-0.98), Human skin (0.95), Blackbody (1.0).
>
> ✅ **Second Law Compliance**: Heat flows from **high to low temperature**; the negative sign in Fourier's Law enforces this.
>
> ✅ **Steady-State Assumption**: Heat flux is constant through every cross-section (no accumulation).
>
> ✅ **Energy Balance**: For composite systems, the rate of heat transfer is identical through all layers under steady state.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – PID Controller**
> A **PID (Proportional–Integral–Derivative) Controller** is a *continuous-domain, feedback-based, error-driven automatic controller* that computes a corrective control signal **u(t)** as a weighted linear combination of three independent error terms — the **present error** (Proportional), the **accumulated past error** (Integral), and the **predicted future error** (Derivative). It is the *de facto industrial standard* controller, accounting for over **95%** of all practical closed-loop control applications in the process industry.

The canonical time-domain equation is:

$$
u(t) \;=\; K_{p}\,e(t) \;+\; K_{i}\!\int_{0}^{t}\! e(\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; K_{d}\,\frac{d\,e(t)}{dt}
$$

where every quantity must be interpreted with proper engineering units (gains in **%/%**, **1/s**, or dimensionless form depending on the transmitter/valve normalisation scheme).

The equivalent **controller transfer function** is:

$$
G_{c}(s) \;=\; \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} \;=\; K_{p}\left(1+\frac{1}{T_{i}s}+T_{d}s\right)
$$

where $K_{p}$ is the **proportional gain (dimensionless)**, $T_{i}$ is the **integral time (seconds or minutes)**, and $T_{d}$ is the **derivative time (seconds or minutes)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "Driving a Car on a Hilly Highway"

Imagine you are driving a car and wish to maintain exactly **80 km/h**.

- **P-action (Steering based on current gap):** The harder you press the accelerator *in proportion* to how far below 80 km/h you currently are. The larger the gap, the firmer the pedal.
- **I-action (Compensating for a long, gentle slope):** Even after the P-action steadies you near 80 km/h, a *long uphill gradient* causes a persistent small error. The I-action *accumulates* this small residual error over time and eventually pushes the speed exactly to 80 km/h, eliminating the *offset*.
- **D-action (Anticipating the brake):** As you *approach* a sharp curve or a downhill, the D-action senses the *rate at which the speed is rising* and eases the accelerator early — preventing overshoot.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Memory Hook:** **P = Present**, **I = Past (cumulative)**, **D = Damping (future prediction).**

## 1.3 Three Standard Controller Actions (KTU Board-Favourite Question)

| Action | Symbol | Hardware / Software Realisation | Industrial Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Proportional | **P** | Pneumatic bellows + flapper-nozzle amplifier | Level, Pressure regulation |
| Proportional + Integral | **PI** | Pneumatic relay with positive feedback bellow | Flow, Temperature |
| Proportional + Integral + Derivative | **PID** | Electronic op-amp summing circuit / DCS function block | Temperature, Composition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "P-only" is rarely used alone:** It produces a permanent **steady-state offset** (also called *droop* or *bias*). The I-action is the **only** mode that mathematically guarantees **zero steady-state error** for a step reference change in a stable Type-0 plant.

## 1.4 Definitions of the Three Tuning Parameters

- **$K_{p}$ (Proportional Gain):** Defines the *strength of present-error response*. Higher $K_{p}$ → faster response, smaller rise time, but **larger overshoot and possible oscillation**.
- **$T_{i}$ (Integral Time / Reset Time):** The time required for the integral term to *duplicate* the contribution of the proportional term for a step error. Smaller $T_{i}$ → stronger integral action.
- **$T_{d}$ (Derivative Time):** The time by which the derivative action *advances* the proportional action along the error trajectory. Larger $T_{d}$ → more damping, but amplifies measurement noise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.1.1 Proportional (P) Mode
1. Sensor measures the **Process Variable (PV)**, e.g., temperature in °C.
2. Comparator computes **error** $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$, where $r$ is setpoint and $y$ is PV.
3. The output of the controller is **linearly scaled** by $K_{p}$: $u_{P}(t) = K_{p}\,e(t)$.
4. The final control element (e.g., a control valve) repositions proportionally.
5. **Limitation:** A *load disturbance* creates a permanent **offset** because a non-zero steady error is required to maintain a non-zero $u$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Origin of Offset in P-only Mode:**
> For a Type-0 plant with DC gain $K_{dc}$, the closed-loop relation gives $e_{ss} = \dfrac{R}{1+K_{p}K_{dc}}$. Only as $K_{p}\to\infty$ does $e_{ss}\to 0$, which is *physically unrealisable* (valve saturates, noise amplifies).

### 2.1.2 Integral (I) Mode
1. Continuously integrates the error over time: $u_{I}(t) = \dfrac{K_{p}}{T_{i}}\!\int_{0}^{t}\!e(\tau)\,d\tau$.
2. As long as *any* error exists, the integral keeps growing, forcing the actuator further.
3. The **only** mode that *integrates* and therefore can drive a constant error to zero.
4. **Limitation:** Pure I is rarely used — it is *too slow* and *poorly damped*.

### 2.1.3 Derivative (D) Mode
1. Operates on the **rate of change** of error: $u_{D}(t) = K_{p}\,T_{d}\,\dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$.
2. Acts **only during transient**; for a constant error, $de/dt = 0$, so D contributes nothing in steady state.
3. Acts as a *predictor* and *damper* — anticipates overshoot.
4. **Limitation:** Highly sensitive to **measurement noise** because differentiation amplifies high-frequency components. A practical PID filter is always used: $G_{D}(s) = \dfrac{K_{p}\,T_{d}\,s}{1+\dfrac{T_{d}}{N}s}$ with $N\in[8,20]$.

### 2.1.4 The Complete PID Block

The standard **non-interacting (parallel / ideal) PID** form is:

$$
G_{c}(s) \;=\; K_{p}\!\left(1+\dfrac{1}{T_{i}s}+T_{d}s\right)
$$

The **interacting (series) PID** form (more common in commercial pneumatic controllers) is:

$$
G_{c}(s) \;=\; K_{p}^{\prime}\!\left(1+\dfrac{1}{T_{i}^{\prime}s}\right)\!\left(1+T_{d}^{\prime}s\right)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Mistake:** Students often write the series form when the question demands parallel form, costing 1–2 marks. Always re-read whether $K_{p}$, $T_{i}$, $T_{d}$ are defined as *independent* (parallel) or *interacting* (series).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $u(t) = K_{p}\!\left[e(t) + \dfrac{1}{T_{i}}\!\int e\,dt + T_{d}\dfrac{de}{dt}\right]$ | Time-domain PID output | $u$ in % of valve travel |
| 2 | $G_{c}(s) = K_{p}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_{i}s} + T_{d}s\right)$ | Ideal parallel PID transfer function | $s$ in rad/s |
| 3 | $K_{i} = \dfrac{K_{p}}{T_{i}}$ | Integral gain | 1/s |
| 4 | $K_{d} = K_{p}\,T_{d}$ | Derivative gain | s |
| 5 | $G_{c}(s) = \dfrac{K_{p}(T_{i}T_{d}s^{2} + T_{i}s + 1)}{T_{i}s}$ | Combined PID numerator form | Useful for root locus |
| 6 | $e_{ss}\big\vert_{P\text{-only}} = \dfrac{R}{1+K_{p}K_{dc}}$ | Steady-state offset for P-only | Type-0 plant |
| 7 | $G_{D,\text{filtered}}(s) = \dfrac{K_{p}T_{d}s}{1+(T_{d}/N)s}$ | Practical (filtered) derivative | $N = 8$ to $20$ |
| 8 | $M_{p}\%,\;t_{s},\;t_{r},\;e_{ss}$ | Time-domain performance specs | 2% settling band standard |
| 9 | $\zeta = \dfrac{-\ln(M_{p}/100)}{\sqrt{\pi^{2}+[\ln(M_{p}/100)]^{2}}}$ | Damping ratio from overshoot | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $K_{u},\;P_{u}$ | Ultimate gain and ultimate period (Z-N) | Found by sustained oscillation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sentinel Value:** In KTU exams, the relation $K_{i}=K_{p}/T_{i}$ and $K_{d}=K_{p}T_{d}$ is a **favourite 1-mark sub-question** in Part A. Memorise it verbatim.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical Industry:** PID maintains crude column overhead temperature to within $\pm 0.5$ °C.
- **Pharmaceutical:** Maintains bioreactor dissolved oxygen (DO) for cell culture survival.
- **Power Plants:** Boiler drum level and superheater steam temperature.
- **Aerospace:** Flight control surface actuators and engine FADEC systems.
- **Robotics & Mechatronics:** Motor position and velocity servo control.
- **HVAC & Smart Buildings:** Zone temperature and airflow regulation.
- **Automotive:** Cruise control, electronic stability control, lane-keeping — all PID-driven.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Reality:** A modern DCS (Distributed Control System) like Honeywell Experion, Emerson DeltaV, or ABB 800xA houses PID as a *function block* (FB). A typical refinery has **5,000–20,000** PID loops running simultaneously.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Time-Domain Equation to Transfer Function $G_{c}(s)$

**Given:**

$$
u(t) \;=\; K_{p}\,e(t) \;+\; \frac{K_{p}}{T_{i}}\!\int_{0}^{t}\!e(\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; K_{p}T_{d}\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 1:** Apply the **Laplace transform** to both sides. Recall that $\mathcal{L}\{e(t)\} = E(s)$, $\mathcal{L}\!\left\{\int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau\right\} = \dfrac{E(s)}{s}$, and $\mathcal{L}\!\left\{\dfrac{de}{dt}\right\} = sE(s)$ (zero initial conditions assumed).

$$
U(s) \;=\; K_{p}\,E(s) \;+\; \frac{K_{p}}{T_{i}}\,\frac{E(s)}{s} \;+\; K_{p}\,T_{d}\,s\,E(s)
$$

**Step 2:** Factor out the common term $E(s)$ on the right-hand side. This is allowed by the distributive law of algebra.

$$
U(s) \;=\; E(s)\!\left[K_{p} + \frac{K_{p}}{T_{i}\,s} + K_{p}\,T_{d}\,s\right]
$$

**Step 3:** Factor out the common scalar $K_{p}$ to obtain the canonical "ideal PID" structure recognised universally in textbooks.

$$
U(s) \;=\; K_{p}\,E(s)\!\left[1 + \frac{1}{T_{i}\,s} + T_{d}\,s\right]
$$

**Step 4:** Recognise that the controller transfer function is defined as $G_{c}(s) = U(s)/E(s)$. Perform the division explicitly.

$$
G_{c}(s) \;=\; \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} \;=\; K_{p}\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{T_{i}\,s} + T_{d}\,s\right)
$$

**Step 5 (Optional – Polynomial Form):** Multiply numerator and denominator by $T_{i}s$ to convert into a single rational expression useful for root-locus and Bode-plot analysis.

$$
G_{c}(s) \;=\; \frac{K_{p}\,T_{i}\,s \;+\; K_{p} \;+\; K_{p}\,T_{i}\,T_{d}\,s^{2}}{T_{i}\,s} \;=\; \frac{K_{p}\!\left(T_{i}T_{d}\,s^{2}+T_{i}\,s+1\right)}{T_{i}\,s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Numerator:** The polynomial $T_{i}T_{d}s^{2}+T_{i}s+1$ is *always* factorable into real zeros when the discriminant is non-negative, i.e., when $T_{i}^{2} \ge 4T_{i}T_{d}$, simplifying to $T_{i}\ge 4T_{d}$. This is the **Bennett–Coughenour rule of thumb** for well-conditioned PID zeros.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steady-State Offset for P-only Control of a Type-0 Plant

**Given:**
- Plant: $G_{p}(s) = \dfrac{K_{dc}}{(1+\tau s)}$ (first-order Type-0 plant)
- Controller: $G_{c}(s) = K_{p}$
- Reference: step of magnitude $R$
- Unity feedback

**Step 1:** Closed-loop transfer function from the standard negative-feedback formula $\dfrac{G_{c}G_{p}}{1+G_{c}G_{p}}$.

$$
\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{K_{p}\,K_{dc}}{1+K_{p}\,K_{dc}} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{\tau}{1+K_{p}K_{dc}}\,s}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **Final Value Theorem (FVT)** to find the steady-state value of $y(t)$ for a step reference of magnitude $R$, i.e., $R(s)=R/s$.

$$
y_{ss} \;=\; \lim_{s\to 0}\,s\,Y(s) \;=\; \lim_{s\to 0}\,s\cdot\frac{R}{s}\cdot\frac{K_{p}K_{dc}}{1+K_{p}K_{dc}} \cdot \frac{1}{1+\frac{\tau}{1+K_{p}K_{dc}}s}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the limit. As $s\to 0$, the third factor $\to 1$, leaving only the DC gain of the closed loop.

$$
y_{ss} \;=\; R\cdot\frac{K_{p}K_{dc}}{1+K_{p}K_{dc}}
$$

**Step 4:** The steady-state error is the difference between the reference and the actual value.

$$
e_{ss} \;=\; R - y_{ss} \;=\; R\left(1 - \frac{K_{p}K_{dc}}{1+K_{p}K_{dc}}\right) \;=\; \frac{R}{1+K_{p}K_{dc}}
$$

**Step 5 (Conclusion):** The offset is non-zero for *any finite* $K_{p}$. Only by adding an integral term — which makes the open-loop transfer function Type-1 — does $e_{ss}\to 0$ for a step input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Significance:** This is the *theorem* that justifies the universal industrial reliance on **PI control** for setpoint regulation in Type-0 plants.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ziegler–Nichols Closed-Loop (Ultimate Cycling) Tuning

**Step 1:** Set $T_{i} = \infty$ (no integral) and $T_{d} = 0$ (no derivative). The controller reduces to pure gain $K_{p}$.

**Step 2:** Increase $K_{p}$ gradually (use the auto/manual station on the DCS) until the closed-loop response exhibits **sustained, constant-amplitude oscillation**. Record this critical gain as the **ultimate gain $K_{u}$** and the oscillation period as the **ultimate period $P_{u}$**.

**Step 3:** Apply the empirical Z-N table (Coughenour–Bennett original, 1942):

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{p,\text{PID}} &= 0.60\,K_{u} \\
T_{i,\text{PID}} &= 0.50\,P_{u} \\
T_{d,\text{PID}} &= 0.125\,P_{u}
\end{aligned}
$$

For PI-only (the most common industrial choice):

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{p,\text{PI}} &= 0.45\,K_{u} \\
T_{i,\text{PI}} &= 0.80\,P_{u}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (Quarter-Amplitude Decay Interpretation):** These settings yield a closed-loop step response with successive peaks decaying to **¼ of the previous peak**, providing a good trade-off between speed and stability.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick — Z-N table digits:** 0.6 / 0.5 / 0.125. *Memorise as 6 : 4 : 1 ratio* for $K_{p}, 1/T_{i}, T_{d}$ relative to the ultimate quantities.
