# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 marks)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between the Proportional, Integral, and Derivative modes of a controller with respect to their effect on steady-state error, transient response, and noise sensitivity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Mode | Effect on Steady-State Error | Effect on Transient Response | Noise Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | Eliminates offset only in Type-1+ plants; for Type-0 plants, a finite offset $\frac{R}{1+K_{p}K_{dc}}$ remains | Reduces rise time, increases overshoot at high $K_{p}$ | Moderate — proportional to noise amplitude |
| **I** | Drives steady-state error to **zero** for step inputs (Type-0 → Type-1 effective) | Slows response; can cause oscillation if $T_{i}$ is too small | Low in steady state; amplifies drift |
| **D** | No effect on steady-state error (contributes only when error changes) | Provides damping, reduces overshoot and settling time | **High** — differentiator amplifies high-frequency noise; hence a filter is mandatory |

> Each correctly identified row = 1 mark.

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is the derivative mode never used alone in process control applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The derivative action produces a non-zero output **only during transients** (when $de/dt \neq 0$). For a constant setpoint with zero error rate, derivative output is zero → no steady-state control. (1 mark)
2. For a slow-changing process variable, derivative output is negligibly small → controller becomes ineffective. (1 mark)
3. It amplifies high-frequency measurement noise, leading to erratic actuator motion and possible equipment damage. (1 mark)

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Compulsory]`

**A process has the open-loop transfer function**

$$
G_{p}(s) \;=\; \frac{2}{(10s+1)(5s+1)}
$$

**A PID controller with transfer function $G_{c}(s) = K_{p}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_{i}s} + T_{d}s\right)$ is used in a unity-feedback configuration.**

#### Part (a) (7 marks)
**Explain the contribution of each term of the PID controller to the closed-loop performance. Show that the addition of the integral term eliminates the steady-state error for a step input, while the derivative term reduces the overshoot.**

#### Part (b) (7 marks)
**Using the Ziegler–Nichols closed-loop tuning method, determine the values of $K_{p}$, $T_{i}$, and $T_{d}$ if the ultimate gain is $K_{u} = 8.5$ and ultimate period is $P_{u} = 12$ s.**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks): Closed-loop error expression.**
The open-loop transfer function with PID is:

$$
L(s) \;=\; G_{c}(s)\,G_{p}(s)
$$

The error $E(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$, and since $Y(s) = L(s)E(s)$, we obtain:

$$
\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{1}{1+L(s)} \;=\; \frac{1}{1+G_{c}(s)G_{p}(s)}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Steady-state error with P-only.**
For $G_{c}(s)=K_{p}$, the open-loop DC gain is $K_{p}G_{p}(0) = 2K_{p}$. By Final Value Theorem for a unit step $R(s)=1/s$:

$$
e_{ss} \;=\; \lim_{s\to 0}\,s\cdot\frac{1}{s}\cdot\frac{1}{1+K_{p}G_{p}(s)} \;=\; \frac{1}{1+2K_{p}} \;\neq\; 0
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Steady-state error with PI.**
Adding the integral term makes the open-loop $L(s) = \dfrac{2K_{p}(T_{i}s+1)}{T_{i}s(10s+1)(5s+1)}$, which is **Type-1** (one pole at origin). For a step input to a Type-1 system, the position error constant $K_{p,\text{pos}} \to \infty$, hence:

$$
e_{ss} \;=\; \frac{1}{1+K_{p,\text{pos}}} \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark): Derivative effect on overshoot.**
The derivative term adds a phase-lead of up to $90^{\circ}$ in the forward path, which increases the phase margin $\phi_{m}$ and **damps the closed-loop poles**, reducing peak overshoot $M_{p}\%$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $K_{u}=8.5$, $P_{u}=12$ s. PID form.

**Step 1 (2 marks): Apply Z-N PID table.**

$$
K_{p} \;=\; 0.60\,K_{u} \;=\; 0.60 \times 8.5 \;=\; 5.1
$$

$$
T_{i} \;=\; 0.50\,P_{u} \;=\; 0.50 \times 12 \;=\; 6.0 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{d} \;=\; 0.125\,P_{u} \;=\; 0.125 \times 12 \;=\; 1.5 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Compute derived gains $K_{i}$ and $K_{d}$.**

$$
K_{i} \;=\; \frac{K_{p}}{T_{i}} \;=\; \frac{5.1}{6.0} \;=\; 0.85 \;\text{s}^{-1}
$$

$$
K_{d} \;=\; K_{p}\,T_{d} \;=\; 5.1 \times 1.5 \;=\; 7.65 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Write the final PID controller transfer function and time-domain equation.**

$$
G_{c}(s) \;=\; 5.1\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{6s} + 1.5s\right) \;=\; \frac{5.1\,(9s^{2}+6s+1)}{6s}
$$

$$
u(t) \;=\; 5.1\,e(t) \;+\; 0.85\!\int_{0}^{t}\!e(\tau)d\tau \;+\; 7.65\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark): Comment on expected performance.**
With these Z-N settings, the closed-loop response is expected to exhibit a **quarter-amplitude decay ratio**, a maximum overshoot of approximately **10–25%**, and a settling time of roughly $1.5\,P_{u} \approx 18$ s.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Alternative Choice]`

#### Part (a) (7 marks)
**Derive the transfer function of an ideal PID controller from its time-domain equation and explain why a low-pass filter is included in the practical implementation of the derivative term.**

#### Part (b) (7 marks)
**For a temperature control loop, a PI controller is tuned with $K_{p}=3.5$ and $T_{i}=240$ s. Calculate the integral gain $K_{i}$, the controller transfer function $G_{c}(s)$, and the offset produced if the loop is operated in P-only mode with the same $K_{p}$ for a unit-step change in setpoint, given that the plant DC gain $K_{dc}=1.2$.**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks): Start from the time-domain PID equation.**

$$
u(t) \;=\; K_{p}\,e(t) \;+\; \frac{K_{p}}{T_{i}}\!\int_{0}^{t}\!e(\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; K_{p}T_{d}\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Apply Laplace transform.**

$$
U(s) \;=\; K_{p}\,E(s) + \frac{K_{p}}{T_{i}s}E(s) + K_{p}T_{d}sE(s) \;=\; K_{p}\!\left(1+\frac{1}{T_{i}s}+T_{d}s\right)E(s)
$$

Therefore:

$$
G_{c}(s) \;=\; \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} \;=\; K_{p}\!\left(1+\frac{1}{T_{i}s}+T_{d}s\right) \;=\; \frac{K_{p}(T_{i}T_{d}s^{2}+T_{i}s+1)}{T_{i}s}
$$

**Step 3 (3 marks): Need for derivative filter.**
The ideal derivative block $G_{D}(s)=K_{p}T_{d}s$ has a magnitude that **grows linearly with frequency**, so at high frequencies (e.g., 50 Hz mains pickup, valve chatter), even a *small* noise component is amplified by an unbounded factor. This causes:
- Excessive actuator wear.
- Erratic control action.
- Possible instability in noisy loops.

The **practical (filtered) derivative** is implemented as:

$$
G_{D,\text{practical}}(s) \;=\; \frac{K_{p}\,T_{d}\,s}{1+\frac{T_{d}}{N}\,s}, \quad N = 8 \text{ to } 20
$$

This adds a high-frequency pole at $\omega = N/T_{d}$ (typically 5–10× the closed-loop bandwidth), saturating the gain at high frequencies and providing **bounded noise amplification**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $K_{p}=3.5$, $T_{i}=240$ s, $K_{dc}=1.2$, unit-step setpoint.

**Step 1 (2 marks): Compute integral gain.**

$$
K_{i} \;=\; \frac{K_{p}}{T_{i}} \;=\; \frac{3.5}{240} \;=\; 0.01458 \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Controller transfer function (PI form).**

$$
G_{c}(s) \;=\; K_{p}\!\left(1+\frac{1}{T_{i}s}\right) \;=\; 3.5\!\left(1+\frac{1}{240s}\right) \;=\; 3.5\cdot\frac{240s+1}{240s} \;=\; \frac{3.5\,(240s+1)}{240s}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Offset with P-only.**
For P-only mode, $G_{c}(s)=K_{p}=3.5$, and the closed-loop error for a unit step is:

$$
e_{ss} \;=\; \frac{1}{1+K_{p}K_{dc}} \;=\; \frac{1}{1+3.5\times 1.2} \;=\; \frac{1}{1+4.2} \;=\; \frac{1}{5.2} \;=\; 0.1923
$$

So the **offset is approximately 19.23%** of the step magnitude.

**Step 4 (1 mark): Conclusion.**
The PI controller, having the integral term, drives this offset to exactly zero — which is the *primary engineering reason* for adding the I-mode to a temperature loop where even small steady-state temperature errors correspond to meaningful product quality deviations.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Frequent Mark-Deduction Traps in PID Questions**
> 1. **Mixing up series and parallel form:** A common 1-mark loss. Always quote the form that matches the question.
> 2. **Forgetting units of $K_{i}$ and $K_{d}$:** $K_{i}$ is in **1/s**; $K_{d}$ is in **s**. Examiners *do* check this.
> 3. **Plugging $K_{p}$ instead of $K_{u}$:** Many students use $K_{p}=0.6$ directly. The correct relation is $K_{p}=0.6\,K_{u}$ — read the *ultimate* values first.
> 4. **Ignoring the derivative filter:** If the question asks for a *practical* PID, a perfect derivative block will lose 1 mark. Always include the filter term $\frac{T_{d}}{N}s$ when $N$ is mentioned.
> 5. **Steady-state error sign convention:** For a *negative* feedback loop with reference increase, the error $e = r - y$ is *positive* when below setpoint. Watch the sign.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions:** Always state "**assuming zero initial conditions**" before applying the Laplace transform.
> 7. **Final numerical rounding:** $e_{ss} = 0.1923$ should be written as **19.23%** or **0.1923** with explicit unit. Random rounding like "0.2" is acceptable only when 3-significant-figure precision is not required.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — PID Controllers (Module 3)**
> - **P-mode:** Output proportional to current error; produces offset in Type-0 plants; reduces rise time.
> - **I-mode:** Output proportional to integral of error; **eliminates offset**; can destabilise if $T_{i}$ too small.
> - **D-mode:** Output proportional to derivative of error; **never used alone**; reduces overshoot; requires noise filter.
> - **Ideal PID equation (time):** $u(t)=K_{p}\!\left[e(t)+\frac{1}{T_{i}}\!\int e\,dt+T_{d}\frac{de}{dt}\right]$.
> - **Ideal PID (Laplace):** $G_{c}(s)=K_{p}\!\left(1+\frac{1}{T_{i}s}+T_{d}s\right)$.
> - **Series form:** $G_{c}(s)=K_{p}^{\prime}\!\left(1+\frac{1}{T_{i}^{\prime}s}\right)\!\left(1+T_{d}^{\prime}s\right)$.
> - **Filtered derivative:** $G_{D}(s)=\dfrac{K_{p}T_{d}s}{1+\frac{T_{d}}{N}s}$, $N\in[8,20]$.
> - **Steady-state offset (P-only):** $e_{ss}=\dfrac{R}{1+K_{p}K_{dc}}$.
> - **Z-N PID tuning:** $K_{p}=0.6K_{u}$, $T_{i}=0.5P_{u}$, $T_{d}=0.125P_{u}$.
> - **Z-N PI tuning:** $K_{p}=0.45K_{u}$, $T_{i}=0.8P_{u}$.
> - **Z-N P tuning:** $K_{p}=0.5K_{u}$.
> - **Rule of thumb:** $T_{i}\ge 4T_{d}$ for well-conditioned PID zeros.
> - **Industrial fact:** **>95%** of all process control loops in industry use PID or its variants (PI most common).
> - **Common plant-controller pairing:** Level/Pressure → P or PI; Flow → PI; Temperature/Composition → PID.
> - **Hardware realisation:** Pneumatic bellows (legacy), Electronic op-amp (analog), DCS function block (modern), PLC PID instruction (IEC 61131-3).
> - **Tuning safety:** Always start in **manual mode**, then switch to **automatic** with $K_{p}$ at *minimum* and gradually increase — never commission a loop at full $K_{u}$.
> - **Performance metrics:** $M_{p}\%$, $t_{r}$, $t_{s}$, $e_{ss}$, IAE, ITAE, ISE.
> - **Stability link:** PID zeros improve phase margin when placed in the *right half of the s-plane* between the dominant plant pole and origin.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: https://nptel.ac.in/courses/117106114 Kinematics of Machinics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT411 - Kinematics of Machinery
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Kinematics of Machines – Cams, Followers and Cam Design Profiles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cam and Follower — Formal Definition

A **cam** is a rotating or sliding mechanical member, usually of irregular shape, which imparts a prescribed motion (displacement, velocity, and acceleration) to another member called the **follower**. The follower maintains continuous contact with the cam profile either by gravity, by a spring, or by a positive mechanical lock. Cams are the principal building blocks of any machine that requires a **timed, repetitive, complex input–output motion** from a simple rotary input.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a cam mechanism is a higher-pair kinematic chain where the contact between the driver (cam) and the driven (follower) is either a point, a line, or a curve — never an area. This places it in the same family as gears and gear-like meshing systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *“A cam is a machine element having a curved outline or a curved groove which, by its oscillation or rotation, gives a predetermined motion to a linked element called the follower.”*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Music Box" of Mechanisms

Imagine a **musical box**. When you turn the handle, a **cylinder with tiny bumps (the cam)** rotates. As each bump lifts a **comb tooth (the follower)**, the tooth plucks a note. Each bump has a different height and shape, and the sequence of bumps creates a melody. The cam does not push the tooth continuously; it pushes it exactly when, how much, and for how long the design wants it to.

**Engineering equivalence:**
- The **cylinder with bumps** is the **cam**.
- The **comb tooth** is the **follower**.
- The **shape and height of each bump** is the **displacement diagram**.
- The **melody** is the **output motion pattern**.

This is exactly what happens inside a car engine's fuel-injection pump, the stitching mechanism of a sewing machine, and the shutter of a DSLR camera — they all use **cams** to convert smooth rotary motion into a **precise, often non-uniform, oscillating or reciprocating output**.

## 1.3 Why Study Cams in Kinematics?

Cams are the **only** machine element that gives the designer **complete geometric freedom** over the output motion. Unlike a slider-crank or a four-bar linkage, where the output motion is fixed once the link lengths are chosen, a cam can be custom-shaped to produce **any arbitrary motion profile** within the geometric limits of the cam radius. This makes them indispensable in:
- **Internal combustion engines** (valve-opening profiles)
- **Automatic lathes and screw machines** (tool-feed timing)
- **Printing and textile machinery** (synchronised indexing)
- **Robotics and automation** (programmed end-effector paths)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard metric used in cam design:**
> - **Base circle (Rb):** the smallest circle drawn tangent to the cam profile. Diameter is **Db = 2Rb**.
> - **Prime circle (Rpr):** the circle passing through the instantaneous point of contact between the cam and follower.
> - **Pitch curve:** the path traced by a point on the follower (for a knife-edge follower) or the centre of the roller (for a roller follower), relative to the cam.
> - **Pressure angle (α):** the angle between the direction of motion of the follower and the normal to the pitch curve at the point of contact.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Examiner Favourite)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Rise** | Movement of the follower *away* from the cam centre |
| **Return** | Movement of the follower *toward* the cam centre |
| **Dwell** | Follower remains stationary while cam rotates |
| **Stroke (s)** | Total displacement of follower during rise (or return) |
| **Base Circle (Rb)** | Reference circle from which cam profile is generated |
| **Pressure Angle (α)** | Governs the side-thrust on the follower |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Cams (KTU Module 4 — High Weightage)

Cams are classified by **shape, type of motion, and follower type**. The KTU board examiner typically asks a **2-mark direct question** asking the student to list any four classifications.

### A. Classification by Cam Shape
1. **Disc / Plate Cam (Eccentric Cam):** The cam rotates about an axis perpendicular to its plane; the follower moves in a plane parallel to the disc. *Most common type* (e.g., IC engine camshafts).
2. **Cylindrical / Drum Cam (Barrel Cam):** A closed cylindrical surface carries the cam track; the follower rides in a groove as the cylinder rotates. Used in printing and copying machines.
3. **Conjugate Cam (Double-Dwell Cam):** Two matched cams rigidly coupled on a single shaft drive a single fork-ended follower; removes the need for a hold-down spring.
4. **Spherical Cam:** Cam surface is part of a sphere; follower oscillates in 3-D. Used in some automatic tool changers.

### B. Classification by Follower Motion
1. **Translating (Sliding) Follower** — follower moves in a straight line (radial or off-axis).
2. **Oscillating (Rocking) Follower** — follower rocks about a fixed pivot.

### C. Classification by Follower Shape
1. **Knife-Edge Follower** — a sharp edge contacts the cam. Theoretically ideal, but wears rapidly.
2. **Flat-Faced Follower** — a flat surface contacts the cam. Low contact stress for slow speeds, used in fuel-pump cams.
3. **Roller Follower** — a small roller rides on the cam profile. The most commonly used in industry because rolling contact minimises wear.
4. **Spherical-Faced (Mushroom) Follower** — a rounded end gives a smoother contact in some oscillating applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why and How:** The choice of follower type is driven by the **loading, speed, accuracy, and wear** considerations. A knife-edge follower is only used in low-speed, low-load situations (e.g., instruments). A roller follower is the **industry default** because it converts the sliding contact at the cam surface into a **rolling contact at the roller periphery**, drastically reducing wear.

## 2.2 Types of Follower Motion (Displacement Diagrams)

The cam designer must choose a **displacement-vs-time function** (or equivalently, displacement-vs-cam-angle function) that the follower must trace during the rise and return portions. The dwell portions are simply horizontal lines. The standard KTU-listed motions are:

### 1. Uniform Velocity (Constant Velocity)
- The follower moves with constant velocity during rise and return.
- **Disadvantage:** Infinite acceleration at the start and end of each stroke — produces severe **infinite jerk and impact**. Rarely used in practice; mainly taught because it is the simplest to construct.

### 2. Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)
- The displacement follows the projection of uniform circular motion onto a diameter.
- **Disadvantage:** Acceleration is finite but **non-zero at the beginning and end of stroke** — still produces a sharp change in force.

### 3. Uniform Acceleration and Retardation (Parabolic / Gravitational Motion)
- The follower accelerates uniformly for the first half of the stroke and decelerates uniformly for the second half.
- **Advantage:** Acceleration is **constant in magnitude** during each half, simplifying force calculations.
- **Disadvantage:** Sudden jump in acceleration at the mid-stroke point — produces infinite jerk there. Hence, it is also called a **constant-acceleration cam**.

### 4. Cycloidal Motion
- The displacement is a cycloidal function: $s = \frac{h}{\pi}\left(\theta - \sin\theta\right)$.
- **Best of all** for general-purpose cams because **acceleration and velocity both start and end at zero**, and the **jerk is also finite and continuous**. **No infinite jerks anywhere** in the cycle.

### 5. 3-4-5 Polynomial Motion (Cubic Polynomial)
- Displacement: $s = h\left[10\left(\frac{t}{t_0}\right)^3 - 15\left(\frac{t}{t_0}\right)^4 + 6\left(\frac{t}{t_0}\right)^5\right]$.
- Velocity starts and ends at zero, acceleration is finite, jerk is also finite. Good for high-speed applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The cycloidal and 3-4-5 polynomial motions dominate high-speed cam design (automotive engines, packaging machinery) because they minimise vibration, noise, and dynamic stress. The uniform-velocity and constant-acceleration profiles are essentially restricted to low-speed cams in instruments and metering devices.

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table is the **complete KTU 2024 cam-design formula bank**. Memorise the column headers and the dimensionless ratios — they apply to any combination of stroke $h$ and cam angle $\phi$.

> **Convention used below:** $h$ = total stroke of follower; $\phi$ = cam angle (in radians) for the rise or return; $\theta = \phi$ for ease.

| Motion Type | Displacement $s$ | Velocity $v = \dfrac{ds}{dt}$ | Max. Velocity $v_{\max}$ | Max. Acceleration $a_{\max}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Uniform Velocity** | $s = h\left(\dfrac{\theta}{\phi}\right)$ | $v = \dfrac{h\omega}{\phi}$ (constant) | $\dfrac{h\omega}{\phi}$ | $\infty$ at the ends |
| **Simple Harmonic** | $s = \dfrac{h}{2}\left[1-\cos\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\phi}\right)\right]$ | $v = \dfrac{\pi h \omega}{2\phi}\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\phi}\right)$ | $\dfrac{\pi h \omega}{2\phi}$ | $\dfrac{\pi^2 h \omega^2}{2\phi^2}$ |
| **Uniform Acceleration & Retardation** (Parabolic) | $s = 2h\left(\dfrac{\theta}{\phi}\right)^2$ for $0 \le \theta \le \phi/2$ ; $s = h\left[1-2\left(1-\dfrac{\theta}{\phi}\right)^2\right]$ for $\phi/2 \le \theta \le \phi$ | $v = \dfrac{4h\omega}{\phi}\cdot\dfrac{\theta}{\phi}$ (first half) | $\dfrac{2h\omega}{\phi}$ | $\dfrac{4h\omega^2}{\phi}$ |
| **Cycloidal** | $s = h\left[\dfrac{\theta}{\phi} - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sin\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\phi}\right)\right]$ | $v = \dfrac{h\omega}{\phi}\left[1-\cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\phi}\right)\right]$ | $2\dfrac{h\omega}{\phi}$ | $\dfrac{2\pi h \omega^2}{\phi^2}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pressure Angle Formula** (KTU favourite — must be memorised):
>
> $$\boxed{\tan\alpha \;=\; \frac{v}{\omega\left(r_0 + s\right)} \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{ds}{d\theta}}{r_0 + s}}$$
>
> where $r_0$ is the base-circle radius, $s$ is the instantaneous follower displacement, and $\omega$ is the cam angular velocity. The **maximum safe pressure angle** for a translating follower is **30°**; for an oscillating follower it is **45°**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) Displacement Equation

This is the **single most-tested derivation** in KTU Module 4. We must derive the displacement of a follower executing SHM, starting from the underlying physics of uniform circular motion.

### Step 1 — Underlying Physical Premise

Consider a point $P$ moving on the circumference of a circle of radius $R$ with **uniform angular velocity** $\omega_p$. The **projection of $P$ on a fixed diameter** of that circle executes simple harmonic motion.

Let us denote the cam angle through which the cam rotates during the rise as $\phi$ (radians), and the corresponding time for the rise as $T$ seconds. The angular velocity of the uniform circular motion of $P$ is then:

$$\omega_p = \frac{\pi}{T}$$

The stroke $h$ of the follower equals the **diameter** of the uniform circular motion, so the radius is:

$$R = \frac{h}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Defining the Geometric Position

After the cam has rotated through an angle $\theta$ (and time $t = \theta/\omega_c$, where $\omega_c$ is the cam's angular speed), the point $P$ has swept an angle $\beta = \omega_p \cdot t$ on its reference circle. From the geometry of the projection:

$$\beta = \omega_p \cdot t = \frac{\pi}{T} \cdot t = \frac{\pi}{\phi} \cdot \theta$$

because $t = \theta / \omega_c$ and $T = \phi / \omega_c$, so the ratio $t/T = \theta/\phi$.

### Step 3 — Writing the Projection

The vertical projection of $P$ (i.e., the follower's position) measured from the centre of the diameter is $R - R\cos\beta$. Adding the initial offset so that displacement $s$ is **zero at $\theta = 0$** and **$h$ at $\theta = \phi$**:

$$s(\theta) = R\left(1 - \cos\beta\right) = \frac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\phi}\right)\right]$$

> **This is the canonical KTU answer** for the displacement equation of a cam with SHM rise.

### Step 4 — Velocity by Differentiation

Differentiate $s$ with respect to time $t$ using the chain rule $\frac{ds}{dt} = \frac{ds}{d\theta}\cdot\frac{d\theta}{dt} = \frac{ds}{d\theta}\cdot\omega$:

$$v(\theta) = \frac{d s}{d t} = \frac{d}{d t}\left\{\frac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\phi}\right)\right]\right\}$$

Apply $\frac{d}{dt} = \omega \frac{d}{d\theta}$:

$$v(\theta) = \frac{h}{2} \cdot \omega \cdot \frac{\pi}{\phi} \cdot \sin\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\phi}\right)$$

$$\boxed{v(\theta) = \frac{\pi h \omega}{2\phi}\sin\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\phi}\right)}$$

### Step 5 — Maximum Velocity

The sine function reaches its maximum value of **1** at $\theta = \phi/2$ (mid-stroke). Hence:

$$v_{\max} = \frac{\pi h \omega}{2\phi}$$

### Step 6 — Acceleration by Second Differentiation

$$a(\theta) = \frac{dv}{dt} = \omega\frac{dv}{d\theta} = \omega \cdot \frac{\pi h \omega}{2\phi} \cdot \frac{\pi}{\phi} \cdot \cos\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\phi}\right)$$

$$\boxed{a(\theta) = \frac{\pi^2 h \omega^2}{2\phi^2}\cos\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\phi}\right)}$$

### Step 7 — Maximum Acceleration

The cosine reaches its maximum magnitude of **1** at $\theta = 0$ (start of stroke) and again at $\theta = \phi$ (end of stroke). Hence:

$$a_{\max} = \frac{\pi^2 h \omega^2}{2\phi^2}$$

> **Note for the KTU valuation key:** Even though $v_{\max}$ occurs at mid-stroke, $a_{\max}$ occurs at the **start and end of the stroke**. This is why an SHM cam produces an audible click and a shock load every time the follower starts and ends its lift — a phenomenon the examiner loves to ask about in the "design commentary" sub-part.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Cycloidal Motion

### Step 1 — Starting Premise

The cycloid is the curve traced by a point on the rim of a circle of radius $r_c$ rolling without slipping along a straight line. If the circle rolls a horizontal distance $\phi$ (the cam-angle equivalent) while the point rises a total height $h$ (the stroke), the displacement of the tracing point projected vertically is:

$$s(\theta) = h\left[\frac{\theta}{\phi} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\phi}\right)\right]$$

### Step 2 — Verification at the Endpoints

At $\theta = 0$ : $s = h\left[0 - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sin(0)\right] = 0$ ✓
At $\theta = \phi$ : $s = h\left[1 - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sin(2\pi)\right] = h$ ✓

### Step 3 — Velocity (Differentiate)

$$v(\theta) = \frac{ds}{dt} = \omega \frac{ds}{d\theta}$$

Compute the derivative of $s$ with respect to $\theta$:

$$\frac{ds}{d\theta} = h\left[\frac{1}{\phi} - \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\phi} \cos\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\phi}\right)\right] = \frac{h}{\phi}\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\phi}\right)\right]$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{v(\theta) = \frac{h\omega}{\phi}\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\phi}\right)\right]}$$

### Step 4 — Maximum Velocity

The bracketed term is maximum when $\cos\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\phi}\right) = -1$, i.e., at $\theta = \phi/2$. The maximum value of the bracket is $1 - (-1) = 2$, so:

$$v_{\max} = \frac{2h\omega}{\phi}$$

### Step 5 — Acceleration (Differentiate Velocity)

$$a(\theta) = \omega \frac{dv/d\theta}{\cdot 1} = \omega \cdot \frac{h\omega}{\phi} \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\phi}\sin\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\phi}\right)$$

$$\boxed{a(\theta) = \frac{2\pi h \omega^2}{\phi^2}\sin\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\phi}\right)}$$

### Step 6 — Maximum Acceleration

The sine reaches its maximum value of 1 at $\theta = \phi/4$ and $\theta = 3\phi/4$. So:

$$a_{\max} = \frac{2\pi h \omega^2}{\phi^2}$$

### Step 7 — Why Cycloidal Is Superior (KTU Favourite Reasoning)

Differentiate acceleration with respect to $t$ to get **jerk** $j$:

$$j(\theta) = \frac{d^3 s}{dt^3} = \omega^3 \cdot \frac{h}{\phi} \cdot \left(\frac{2\pi}{\phi}\right)^2 \cos\left(\frac{2\pi\theta}{\phi}\right)$$

This jerk is **finite everywhere** (no infinite jumps), unlike uniform-velocity and constant-acceleration cams. Therefore the **cycloidal cam is the most vibration-free and is the preferred choice for high-speed applications**.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Full KTU 14-Mark Style

> **Problem (KTU-style, comprehensive):**
> A cam rotates at **600 rpm** and provides the following motion to a roller follower:
> – **Dwell** for the first $60°$ of cam rotation.
> – **Rise** of $30\text{ mm}$ during the next $90°$ with **simple harmonic motion**.
> – **Dwell** for the next $30°$.
> – **Return** of $30\text{ mm}$ during the next $120°$ with **uniform acceleration and retardation**.
> – **Dwell** for the remaining $60°$ of the cycle.
>
> Determine: (a) the maximum velocity of the follower during rise, (b) the maximum velocity of the follower during return, (c) the maximum acceleration during rise, (d) the maximum acceleration during return, and (e) construct the displacement diagram.

### Part (a) — Maximum Velocity during Rise (SHM)

**Given:** $h = 30\text{ mm} = 0.030\text{ m}$, $\phi_{\text{rise}} = 90° = \pi/2 \text{ rad}$, $N = 600\text{ rpm}$.

**Step 1 — Compute cam angular velocity:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 600}{60} = 20\pi \text{ rad/s} \approx 62.832 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply SHM maximum velocity formula:**

$$v_{\max}^{\text{rise}} = \frac{\pi h \omega}{2 \phi_{\text{rise}}} = \frac{\pi \times 0.030 \times 20\pi}{2 \times (\pi/2)}$$

$$v_{\max}^{\text{rise}} = \frac{\pi \times 0.030 \times 20\pi}{\pi} = 0.030 \times 20\pi \times \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2 = 0.030 \times 20 = 0.600 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{\max}^{\text{rise}} = 0.600 \text{ m/s} = 600 \text{ mm/s}}$$

### Part (b) — Maximum Velocity during Return (Parabolic)

**Given:** $h = 30\text{ mm}$, $\phi_{\text{return}} = 120° = 2\pi/3 \text{ rad}$.

**Step 1 — Apply the constant-acceleration cam maximum velocity formula** ($v_{\max} = \frac{2h\omega}{\phi}$):

$$v_{\max}^{\text{return}} = \frac{2 \times 0.030 \times 20\pi}{2\pi/3} = \frac{0.060 \times 20\pi \times 3}{2\pi} = \frac{0.060 \times 60}{2} = 1.800 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{\max}^{\text{return}} = 1.800 \text{ m/s} = 1800 \text{ mm/s}}$$

### Part (c) — Maximum Acceleration during Rise (SHM)

$$a_{\max}^{\text{rise}} = \frac{\pi^2 h \omega^2}{2 \phi_{\text{rise}}^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 0.030 \times (20\pi)^2}{2 \times (\pi/2)^2}$$

Compute step-by-step:

$$\frac{\pi^2 \times 0.030 \times 400\pi^2}{2 \times \pi^2/4} = \frac{1200 \pi^4 \times 0.030 \cdot 400}{(\pi^2/2)}$$

Let us re-evaluate carefully:

$$a_{\max}^{\text{rise}} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 0.030 \times 400\pi^2}{2 \times \pi^2/4} = \frac{0.030 \times 400\pi^4}{0.5\pi^2} = \frac{0.030 \times 400 \times \pi^2}{0.5} = \frac{0.030 \times 800\pi^2}{1}$$

$$= 24 \pi^2 \approx 24 \times 9.8696 \approx 236.87 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$\boxed{a_{\max}^{\text{rise}} \approx 236.9 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

### Part (d) — Maximum Acceleration during Return (Parabolic)

**Given:** parabolic cam, $a_{\max} = \frac{4h\omega^2}{\phi}$:

$$a_{\max}^{\text{return}} = \frac{4 \times 0.030 \times (20\pi)^2}{2\pi/3} = \frac{0.120 \times 400\pi^2}{2\pi/3} = \frac{48\pi^2 \times 3}{2\pi} = 72\pi$$

$$\boxed{a_{\max}^{\text{return}} \approx 226.19 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

### Part (e) — Displacement Diagram (Construction Recipe)

| Cam Angle (°) | Phase | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 0 → 60 | Dwell | $s = 0$, horizontal line |
| 60 → 150 | Rise (SHM) | Plot $\dfrac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi(\theta - 60°)}{90°}\right)\right]$ |
| 150 → 180 | Dwell | $s = h = 30\text{ mm}$, horizontal line |
| 180 → 300 | Return (Parabolic) | Two parabolic arcs, peak slope at mid-stroke |
| 300 → 360 | Dwell | $s = 0$, horizontal line |

The completed diagram will be a closed loop returning to the base line, with smooth SHM curves on the rise and parabolic-curved return.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cam–Follower System — Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Driver: Prime Mover] -->|Torque| B[Cam Shaft]
    B -->|Rotates at ω| C[Cam Disc with Profile]
    C -->|Contact at point P| D[Follower Translates or Oscillates]
    D -->|Stroke s| E[Output Mechanism: Valve, Tool, etc.]
    F[Hold-Down Spring] -->|Maintains contact| D
    G[Lubrication System] -.->|Reduces wear| C
    C -->|Pressure angle α| H[Side Thrust on Guide]

    classDef input fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef auxiliary fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#330099,color:#000

    class A input
    class B,C,D process
    class E output
    class F,G,H auxiliary
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The energy flow goes from the driver → cam → follower → output. The hold-down spring and lubrication are auxiliary subsystems that **ensure functional integrity**. The pressure angle $\alpha$ appears as a side-thrust on the follower guide, which is a **design constraint** (must be kept below the safe limit).

## 4.2 Cam Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Cam Mechanism] --> Q1{Motion of Cam?}
    Q1 -->|Rotating| Rot[Rotating Cam]
    Q1 -->|Translating| Trans[Translating Cam]

    Rot --> R1{Contact Type?}
    R1 -->|Knife-edge| RE1[Knife-edge Follower]
    R1 -->|Roller| RE2[Roller Follower]
    R1 -->|Flat-faced| RE3[Flat-faced Follower]
    R1 -->|Spherical| RE4[Mushroom Follower]

    RE1 -->|Wears fast| W1[Low-speed use only]
    RE2 -->|Industrial std| W2[Standard for engines]
    RE3 -->|Compact| W3[Fuel-pump cams]
    RE4 -->|Smooth contact| W4[High-load oscillating]

    Trans --> T1{Use Case}
    T1 --> T1a[Printing machines]
    T1 --> T1b[Drum-type control]
    T1 --> T1c[Specialty automation]
```

## 4.3 Cam Profile Construction (Geometric Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Draw Base Circle of radius Rb] --> P2[Step 2: Mark rise dwell return dwell angles on the base circle]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Lay off displacement s perpendicular to cam radius for each cam angle θ]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: If roller follower - lay off roller radius inward from each displacement point to get pitch curve]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Draw a smooth curve through the pitch points to obtain the pitch curve]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Offset the roller radius outward to obtain the actual cam profile]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Verify maximum pressure angle ≤ 30 deg for translating follower]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Construction Sequence (Memorise the order):**
> 1. Base circle → 2. Mark cam angles for each phase → 3. Lay off displacements (s = h × dimensionless ratio) → 4. **For roller follower, lay off roller radius inward to get pitch curve** → 5. Draw pitch curve → 6. Offset outward by roller radius for cam profile. Skipping the inward offset for a roller follower is a **favourite KTU valuation deduction**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (with Model Answers)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Set A]**
> Define the term **"base circle"** of a cam. State the relationship between the base-circle radius and the prime-circle radius for a roller follower.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **base circle** of a cam is the **smallest circle that can be drawn tangent to the cam profile**, with its centre coinciding with the axis of rotation of the cam. The radius of this circle is denoted by $R_b$ (or $R_0$) and is the reference from which the cam profile is constructed.

For a **roller follower** of radius $R_r$, the **prime circle** is the circle that passes through the **centres of the rollers** as the follower traces its path on the cam. The prime-circle radius is **greater** than the base-circle radius:

$$R_{\text{prime}} = R_b + R_r$$

Hence the base circle is tangent to the cam profile itself, while the prime circle is tangent to the pitch curve. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Set B]**
> List any **four classifications of followers** used in cam mechanisms.

**Model Answer (3 marks — one mark per correct pair, max three marks if all four in one line):**

1. **Knife-edge follower** — a sharp edge contacts the cam.
2. **Roller follower** — a roller contacts the cam profile.
3. **Flat-faced follower** — a flat face contacts the cam.
4. **Spherical-faced (mushroom) follower** — a hemispherical face contacts the cam.
5. **Translating follower** — moves in a straight line.
6. **Oscillating follower** — rocks about a fixed pivot. **(Any four — 3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A — Comprehensive Design Problem
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Set A]**
> A cam rotates uniformly at **300 rpm** and operates a translating roller follower through the following sequence:
> (i) Dwell for $0°$ to $60°$ of cam rotation.
> (ii) **Rise** of $40\text{ mm}$ during $60°$ to $180°$ with **simple harmonic motion**.
> (iii) **Dwell** for $180°$ to $240°$.
> (iv) **Return** of $40\text{ mm}$ during $240°$ to $360°$ with **uniform acceleration and retardation**.
>
> **(a)** Draw the **displacement diagram** of the follower. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Calculate the **maximum velocity and maximum acceleration** during the rise and return strokes. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Construction table for the rise (SHM, 0° to 120° of the rise interval):**

| Rise angle $\beta$ (°) | $\cos\beta$ | $s = \dfrac{h}{2}(1 - \cos\beta)$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1.000 | 0.00 mm |
| 15 | 0.966 | 0.68 mm |
| 30 | 0.866 | 2.68 mm |
| 45 | 0.707 | 5.86 mm |
| 60 | 0.500 | 10.00 mm |
| 75 | 0.259 | 14.82 mm |
| 90 | 0.000 | 20.00 mm |
| 105 | −0.259 | 25.18 mm |
| 120 | −0.500 | 30.00 mm |

**Construction table for the return (parabolic, 0° to 120° of the return interval):**

| Return angle $\gamma$ (°) | Ratio $r$ | $s = h(1 - 2(1-r)^2)$ for $r \le 0.5$ ; $s = h(2(r-1)^2)$ for $r \ge 0.5$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.000 | 40.00 mm |
| 15 | 0.125 | 39.06 mm |
| 30 | 0.250 | 36.25 mm |
| 60 (= 0.5) | 0.500 | 30.00 mm |
| 90 | 0.750 | 16.25 mm |
| 120 (= 1.0) | 1.000 | 0.00 mm |

**Plot the curve** on a graph of follower displacement (y-axis) versus cam angle (x-axis) for 0° → 360°. Dwell segments are horizontal lines at $s = 0$ and $s = 40$ mm. **(7 marks — construction table 3, accuracy 2, axes and labels 2)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $h = 0.040$ m, $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 10\pi$ rad/s, $\phi_{\text{rise}} = 120° = 2\pi/3$ rad, $\phi_{\text{return}} = 120° = 2\pi/3$ rad.

**During rise (SHM):**

$$v_{\max}^{\text{rise}} = \frac{\pi h \omega}{2\phi_{\text{rise}}} = \frac{\pi \times 0.040 \times 10\pi}{2 \times 2\pi/3} = \frac{0.400 \pi^2}{4\pi/3} = \frac{0.400 \times 3\pi}{4} = 0.300\pi \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{\max}^{\text{rise}} \approx 0.942 \text{ m/s}}$$

$$a_{\max}^{\text{rise}} = \frac{\pi^2 h \omega^2}{2\phi_{\text{rise}}^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 0.040 \times 100\pi^2}{2 \times 4\pi^2/9} = \frac{4\pi^4 \times 0.040 \times 9}{8\pi^2}$$

$$= \frac{0.040 \times 9 \pi^2}{2} = 0.180 \pi^2$$

$$\boxed{a_{\max}^{\text{rise}} \approx 1.776 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

**During return (parabolic):**

$$v_{\max}^{\text{return}} = \frac{2h\omega}{\phi_{\text{return}}} = \frac{2 \times 0.040 \times 10\pi}{2\pi/3} = \frac{0.800\pi \times 3}{2\pi} = 1.200 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{\max}^{\text{return}} = 1.200 \text{ m/s}}$$

$$a_{\max}^{\text{return}} = \frac{4h\omega^2}{\phi_{\text{return}}} = \frac{4 \times 0.040 \times 100\pi^2}{2\pi/3} = \frac{16\pi^2 \times 3}{2\pi} = 24\pi$$

$$\boxed{a_{\max}^{\text{return}} \approx 75.40 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

> **Comparison insight (for design commentary):** The acceleration during the return stroke is roughly **42 times** larger than during the rise. This is because the return must be completed in the same angular span (120°), but uses a different (parabolic) motion that is more "aggressive" at the end-points. The design would benefit from **slowing down the return** (larger $\phi_{\text{return}}$) to reduce dynamic loads. **(1 mark reserved for design insight)**

### Question B — Alternative Comprehensive Problem
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Set B]**
> A cam with an **oscillating roller follower** is to be designed. The follower rocks through an angle of $30°$ during a rise of $40°$ of cam rotation, executing **cycloidal motion**. The follower length is $100$ mm, and the cam rotates at $240$ rpm.
>
> **(a)** Derive the displacement, velocity, and acceleration equations for a cycloidal motion cam. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Plot the **displacement diagram** and determine the **maximum velocity and acceleration** of the follower tip. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

> **Note:** For an oscillating follower, displacement is angular ($\theta_f$), not linear. We use $\theta_f$ in place of $s$.

**Step 1 — Cycloidal displacement:**

The cycloidal motion law in terms of the follower angle $\theta_f$ is:

$$\boxed{\theta_f(\beta) = \theta_{f,\max}\left[\frac{\beta}{\phi} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin\left(\frac{2\pi\beta}{\phi}\right)\right]}$$

where $\beta$ is the cam angle and $\phi$ is the cam angle for the rise.

**Step 2 — Angular velocity** (differentiation):

$$\omega_f(\beta) = \frac{d\theta_f}{dt} = \frac{\theta_{f,\max}\omega}{\phi}\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{2\pi\beta}{\phi}\right)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Maximum angular velocity:** at $\beta = \phi/2$, bracket = 2, so:

$$\omega_{f,\max} = \frac{2\theta_{f,\max}\omega}{\phi}$$

**Step 4 — Angular acceleration** (second derivative):

$$\alpha_f(\beta) = \frac{2\pi\theta_{f,\max}\omega^2}{\phi^2}\sin\left(\frac{2\pi\beta}{\phi}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Maximum angular acceleration:** at $\beta = \phi/4$ and $3\phi/4$, sine = 1, so:

$$\alpha_{f,\max} = \frac{2\pi\theta_{f,\max}\omega^2}{\phi^2}$$

**(7 marks — full derivation chain above)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $\theta_{f,\max} = 30° = \pi/6$ rad, $\phi = 40° = 2\pi/9$ rad, $\omega = 2\pi \times 240 / 60 = 8\pi$ rad/s, follower length $L = 100$ mm.

**Step 1 — Maximum angular velocity:**

$$\omega_{f,\max} = \frac{2 \times (\pi/6) \times 8\pi}{2\pi/9} = \frac{(\pi/3) \times 8\pi \times 9}{2\pi} = \frac{8\pi \times 9}{6} = 12\pi \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to linear tip velocity:**

$$v_{f,\max} = \omega_{f,\max} \times L = 12\pi \times 0.100 = 1.2\pi \approx \boxed{3.770 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum angular acceleration:**

$$\alpha_{f,\max} = \frac{2\pi \times (\pi/6) \times (8\pi)^2}{(2\pi/9)^2} = \frac{2\pi \times \pi/6 \times 64\pi^2}{4\pi^2/81} = \frac{128\pi^4/6 \times 81}{4\pi^2}$$

$$= \frac{128 \times 81 \pi^2}{24} = 432\pi^2 \approx 4263.6 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

**Step 4 — Linear tip acceleration:**

$$a_{f,\max} = \alpha_{f,\max} \times L = 432\pi^2 \times 0.100 \approx \boxed{426.4 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

**Displacement diagram:** Plot $\theta_f$ vs $\beta$ from 0 to 40° using the cycloidal formula. The curve will be S-shaped with zero slope at both ends and a single peak of slope at mid-stroke. **(7 marks — equation 3, max values 2, plot 2)**

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-Mark Mark-Deductions — Read This Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the inward offset for roller-follower cam construction (–2 marks):** When constructing the cam profile for a roller follower, the displacement must be **laid off from the roller centre** to get the pitch curve. Skipping the step of "lay off the roller radius inward" is the single most common error.
> 2. **Confusing $v_{\max}$ and $a_{\max}$ locations (–1 mark):** For SHM, $v_{\max}$ occurs at the **mid-stroke**, but $a_{\max}$ occurs at the **start and end of the stroke**. Writing them at the same point loses the mark.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert $N$ (rpm) to $\omega$ (rad/s) (–1 mark):** Many students substitute $\omega = 300$ instead of $\omega = 10\pi$ and get an answer that is off by a factor of $2\pi$.
> 4. **Unit mismatch (–1 mark):** Stroke $h$ should be in metres when $\omega$ is in rad/s, to get $v$ in m/s and $a$ in m/s².
> 5. **Not writing units in the final answer (–0.5 mark):** Always write **m/s** or **mm/s** and **m/s²** explicitly.
> 6. **Cycloidal cam: forgetting the finite-jerk property (–1 mark):** If the question asks "which cam motion is preferred for high-speed applications?", the answer **must explicitly mention that jerk is finite and continuous**, not just that the cam is "smooth".

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Cam = driver** (irregular shape); **Follower = driven** (translating or oscillating).
- [x] Three classifications: **by cam shape, by follower motion, by follower type**.
- [x] Four standard follower types: **knife-edge, roller, flat-faced, mushroom (spherical-faced)**.
- [x] Two principal follower motions: **translating (radial, off-axis)** and **oscillating (rocking)**.
- [x] **Five standard displacement diagrams** in KTU Module 4: uniform velocity, SHM, parabolic (uniform accel/retard), cycloidal, and 3-4-5 polynomial.
- [x] **Base circle $R_b$** is the smallest circle tangent to the cam profile. **Prime circle** = $R_b + R_r$ for roller follower.
- [x] **Pressure angle $\alpha$** is the angle between the follower's line of motion and the normal to the pitch curve at the point of contact. **Safe limit: 30° (translating) and 45° (oscillating).**
- [x] **Pressure-angle formula:** $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{ds/d\theta}{r_0 + s}$ (radial translating follower).
- [x] **SHM displacement:** $s = \dfrac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\phi}\right)\right]$.
- [x] **SHM $v_{\max}$:** $\dfrac{\pi h \omega}{2\phi}$ (at mid-stroke).
- [x] **SHM $a_{\max}$:** $\dfrac{\pi^2 h \omega^2}{2\phi^2}$ (at start and end of stroke).
- [x] **Parabolic displacement (first half):** $s = 2h\left(\dfrac{\theta}{\phi}\right)^2$ for $0 \le \theta \le \phi/2$.
- [x] **Parabolic $v_{\max}$:** $\dfrac{2h\omega}{\phi}$ (at mid-stroke).
- [x] **Parabolic $a_{\max}$:** $\dfrac{4h\omega^2}{\phi}$ (during the first half — constant magnitude).
- [x] **Cycloidal displacement:** $s = h\left[\dfrac{\theta}{\phi} - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sin\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\phi}\right)\right]$.
- [x] **Cycloidal $v_{\max}$:** $\dfrac{2h\omega}{\phi}$ (at mid-stroke — same magnitude as parabolic's $v_{\max}$ but in different motion).
- [x] **Cycloidal $a_{\max}$:** $\dfrac{2\pi h \omega^2}{\phi^2}$ (at $\theta = \phi/4$ and $3\phi/4$).
- [x] **Cycloidal is best for high-speed cams** because **acceleration, velocity, and jerk are all continuous and finite** at the stroke boundaries.
- [x] **Cam profile construction sequence:** Base circle → lay off angular intervals → lay off displacements → for roller follower, lay off roller radius inward to get pitch curve → draw pitch curve → offset outward by roller radius to get cam profile.
- [x] **Always convert rpm to rad/s** using $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ before substituting.
- [x] **Use SI units throughout** ($h$ in m, $r_0$ in m, $\omega$ in rad/s → $v$ in m/s, $a$ in m/s²).
- [x] **Trade-off rule:** Uniform velocity is simplest but worst dynamically. Cycloidal and 3-4-5 polynomial are most expensive to design but are mandatory for high-speed, low-vibration applications.
